transition_candidate_class_identifiers.summary: A bounded runtime-adjacent transition whose meaning reaches lifecycle, replay, snapshot, isolation, coexistence, or hotswap-boundary continuity.
A boundary-sensitive transition whose meaning depends on trust posture, trust prerequisites, signer or verifier acceptance, or trust-bearing refusal and acceptance rules.
A transition whose continuity meaning depends on revocation declaration, propagation posture, containment, restoration, or supersession remaining reconstructable.
A bounded release or control-plane transition that changes selection, visibility, staged availability, or runtime-adjacent operational posture in a boundary-sensitive way.
A later transition candidate whose meaning would eventually depend on authority handoff, proof-anchor quorum, distributed replay continuity, or state-partition movement.
A bounded high-urgency or high-sensitivity transition used for containment, restoration, or supersession where partial or blocked movement must remain visible.

receipt_class_identifiers.summary: Canonical evidence for intended boundary-sensitive transition scope, review posture, and upstream doctrine inputs.
Canonical evidence for an explicitly bounded live-adjacent or runtime-adjacent transition outcome.
Canonical evidence for a cutover-relevant transition whose meaning depends on trust posture, trust prerequisites, or trust-bearing refusal and acceptance rules.
Canonical evidence for revocation-linked boundary movement, including declaration linkage, propagation posture, containment posture, and restoration or supersession posture.
Canonical evidence for stronger continuity anchors used to prove reconstructability across boundary movement.
Canonical evidence for boundary-sensitive rehearsal, simulation, preflight, or proof-gathering outcomes that remain explicitly non-live.
Canonical evidence that a boundary-sensitive transition was blocked, refused, under-specified, or otherwise not lawfully completed.

provenance_generalization_categories.summary: Preserve stronger sequencing and linkage anchors than ordinary operator provenance requires.
Preserve explicit boundary-sensitive state references when realizations, cutover horizons, or live-adjacent scopes matter.
Preserve trust and revocation posture continuity where acceptance or refusal meaning changes across boundary movement.
Preserve archive, mirror, release-index, and release-identity continuity without collapsing those concepts into one proof surface.
Preserve whether a transition was intended, partial, bounded, completed, blocked, or refused.
Preserve the difference between live boundary movement and non-live rehearsal, simulation, or proof-gathering.
Preserve actor posture, authority posture, review posture, and doctrine inputs across the transition history.

trust_release_archive_runtime_dependence: Ordinary operator transaction receipts remain the baseline and live-cutover receipt classes are a stronger extension layer rather than a replacement.
Trust-transition prerequisites remain upstream and generalized receipts must not claim trust readiness that has not been constitutionally admitted.
Revocation continuity must remain visible in generalized receipts rather than disappearing into local traces or mirror changes.
Release identity, release contract profile, release-index semantics, exact target context, and channel posture remain upstream truth.
Archive presence may support reconstructability but is not by itself cutover provenance continuity.
Mirror visibility may support availability interpretation but is not cutover proof.
Any runtime-sensitive receipt remains subordinate to lifecycle, replay, snapshot, isolation, coexistence, and hotswap-boundary doctrine.
This doctrine does not prove live cutover maturity or distributed-authority readiness.

invalidity_failure_categories.summary: A supposed live-boundary transition is evidenced only by ordinary logs or traces.
An ordinary operator receipt was used where stronger boundary-sensitive continuity anchors were required.
A partial, bounded, blocked, or interrupted transition was misrepresented as completed.
The evidence exists only in local tracing, console, or derived surfaces with no canonical generalized receipt.
The transition lacks stronger continuity anchors needed for reconstructable live-boundary meaning.
Release identity, release contract profile, release-index semantics, or exact target context is incomplete or absent.
Trust or revocation posture is missing, contradictory, or not composable with the generalized receipt model.
Archive or mirror state was treated as sufficient evidence of cutover continuity or proof.
Non-live rehearsal or proof-gathering evidence was misrepresented as live transition evidence.
The receipt or provenance claim exceeds current lifecycle, replay, snapshot, isolation, coexistence, or hotswap maturity.
The evidence model assumes distributed handoff or quorum continuity that has not yet been frozen constitutionally.
The boundary-sensitive history cannot be reconstructed canonically across intent, proof, refusal, and result.

canonical: upstream operator receipt, trust, release, runtime, and hotswap doctrine
canonical generalized receipt and provenance records where later doctrine explicitly defines them
canonical trust policy, release identity, and release-index artifacts with intact provenance

prohibited_receipt_provenance_shapes: local log treated as canonical cutover receipt
ordinary operator receipt treated as sufficient for live-boundary transition
mirror disappearance treated as cutover proof
changelog prose treated as provenance continuity
receipt continuity assumed because old artifacts still exist
dashboard, filename, or console output treated as canonical boundary truth
trust or revocation continuity recorded with no boundary-sensitive anchors
doctrine existence treated as implementation maturity
